name: master ragu
instructions:
FOUR VARIANTS UNDER ONE PASTA-RAGU SHAPE. All share: tomato base + a protein (real or faux meat, or lentils) + acid (wine or wine vinegar) + soffrito-style aromatics + pasta. Variant A is the canonical bolognese and gets the headline treatment.

=== VARIANT A — MARCELLA BOLOGNESE (CANONICAL) ===

The load-bearing recipe in this family. Marcella Hazan, Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking. If 'italian ragu' means one thing in this cookbook, it's this one.

INGREDIENTS:
- vegetable oil + butter (the fat)
- yellow onions, celery, carrots — finely diced soffrito
- ground beef (or impossible meat as the substitute)
- milk
- nutmeg
- white wine
- canned tomatoes
- black pepper
- pasta (traditionally tagliatelle, not spaghetti)
- parmesan cheese for serving

TECHNIQUE — MARCELLA'S NON-NEGOTIABLES:
1. Cook the beef ONLY until it loses raw color. Do NOT brown it. Browning gives a different (and lesser) ragu.
2. Milk goes in BEFORE wine. The milk coats the meat and protects it from the wine acid; reversing the order changes the texture.
3. Bare-simmer for THREE+ HOURS uncovered. Splash in water if it dries out. The long uncovered simmer is what makes it a bolognese; cutting time turns it into a generic meat sauce.
4. Fat should separate at the end. That's the visual signal you're done.

=== VARIANT B — EGGPLANT RAGU (VEGAN) ===

- Sauté finely diced yellow onions and shallots in olive oil with garlic.
- Add diced eggplant and portabella mushrooms; cook down until soft and browned.
- Add ground vegan meat, break it up, cook through.
- Stir in canned tomatoes, porcini mushroom powder, nutritional yeast, red wine vinegar. Simmer until thickened.
- Toss with pasta.

=== VARIANT C — MUSHROOM LENTIL RAGU (VEGETARIAN) ===

- Brown mushrooms HARD in olive oil — get real color on them. Set aside.
- Sauté yellow onions in the same pan. Deglaze with red wine; reduce.
- Add lentils, canned tomatoes, and enough water to cover the lentils. Simmer until lentils are tender.
- Return mushrooms to the pan; warm through.
- Finish OFF HEAT with parmesan, nutritional yeast, basil, and parsley. Salt and black pepper to taste.
- Toss with pasta.

=== VARIANT D — FIRE ROASTED MEATBALLS ===

- Shape impossible meat into meatballs and brown in olive oil.
- Add FIRE ROASTED canned tomatoes (not regular — the fire-roasted character is the dish's signature), red wine vinegar, fennel seeds, bay leaf.
- Simmer until thickened.
- Toss with pasta.
notes:
THE RAGU SHAPE — WHAT THESE FOUR HAVE IN COMMON:
1. A tomato base (canned tomatoes in all four; fire-roasted in variant D specifically).
2. A protein: real meat (A), faux meat (B, D), or lentils (C).
3. An acid: white wine (A), red wine vinegar (B, D), or red wine (C). Pick one per variant — don't mix or substitute across variants.
4. Soffrito-style aromatics: yellow onion is always in; celery/carrot/shallots/garlic vary.
5. Served over pasta.

DISTINCTION FROM LENTIL-MUSHROOM-STEW: that dish is a stew, not a ragu — broth-forward, served as a soup, explicitly anti-canned-tomato and anti-tomato-paste, uses stock not pasta. It has overlapping headline ingredients with variant C but contradictory rules. Don't merge the two.

WHY BOLOGNESE GETS THE HEADLINE: variant A is the canonical italian ragu — Marcella Hazan's recipe is the cookbook's reference point. The three non-negotiables (don't brown the beef, milk before wine, three-hour uncovered simmer) define what 'bolognese' means here. Other variants borrow the ragu shape but don't try to be bolognese — they're distinct dishes, not vegetarianized bolognese.

WHY MILK IS IN BOLOGNESE BUT NOT THE OTHERS: milk's role is to coat the beef and tenderize it during the long simmer. The vegan/vegetarian variants don't have raw beef to protect, and the acid stress (red wine vinegar in B and D, red wine in C) is built into the recipe rather than fought against. Adding milk to variants B/C/D doesn't help — it muddies the tomato.

FAUX MEAT SUBSTITUTIONS:
- Variant A (bolognese): impossible meat works as a substitute for ground beef. Don't substitute ground vegan meat for ground beef in bolognese — impossible has the right fat profile, ground vegan meat reads off.
- Variant B (eggplant): ground vegan meat is the canonical choice. Impossible also works but is overkill.
- Variant D (meatballs): impossible meat shaped into meatballs. The shaping matters — the meatball format is the distinguishing texture; without it, this becomes generic vegan ragu.

FIRE ROASTED TOMATOES ARE FOR VARIANT D ONLY: don't reach for fire-roasted in the other variants. Variant A (bolognese) wants regular canned tomatoes — fire-roasted reads too smoky against the milk-and-nutmeg base. Variants B and C also want regular canned. Fire-roasted is the signature of variant D; using it elsewhere is wrong.

NO BASIL OR PARSLEY IN BOLOGNESE OR EGGPLANT: variants A and B don't take fresh herb finishes well. Bolognese's long simmer and milk-and-nutmeg base is its own register; basil/parsley on top reads as an addition that doesn't help. Eggplant ragu is similar — porcini powder and nutritional yeast do the umami work, fresh herbs are a distraction. Variant C (mushroom lentil) DOES want basil and parsley off heat — the lighter wine-and-lentil base is brightened by them.

TAGLIATELLE OVER SPAGHETTI FOR VARIANT A: marcella was explicit that bolognese is for tagliatelle, not spaghetti. The wide flat noodle holds the meat sauce; spaghetti slides under it. Variants B, C, D are more forgiving — penne or other shapes work fine.
